← all tutorials

Distributed Systems Fundamentals — interactive DISTRIBUTED_SYSTEMS.md distributed_systems.py

The building blocks under every consensus, replication, and consistency algorithm: the failure model (crash / omission / byzantine → 2f+1 vs 3f+1), time & clocks (drift, Lamport, vector clocks), and the FLP / partial-synchrony timing reality. Drag the sliders to watch clock skew grow and a vector clock reveal concurrency that a Lamport scalar hides. All numbers are recomputed in JS from the identical trace and formulas as distributed_systems.py.

① Failure model explorer — crash / omission / byzantine → 2f+1 vs 3f+1

What to do: pick a failure class and a number of faults f. The ring shows a minimal cluster that tolerates f faults of that class. Crash tolerance needs N=2f+1 (any two majorities overlap ≥1 node); byzantine tolerance needs N=3f+1 (honest ≥2f+1 must outvote f traitors AND overlap in ≥f+1 honest nodes).
Crash ⊂ omission ⊂ byzantine — each harder class is a superset, so it costs more replicas. The quorum overlap fact (two majorities of N share ≥ 2·⌈(N+1)/2⌉ − N nodes) is the single reason majority quorums stay safe.

② Clock synchronization & causality — drift, Lamport, vector clocks

What to do: drag rho (drift rate) and T (interval) to watch the max clock skew 2·ρ·T blow up — the reason raw wall-clocks can't order events. Then step through the pinned 3-process trace (a,b,c,d,y causal chain; x concurrent): toggle Lamport vs vector clock. The scalar gives x the same L=1 as a (hiding that they are concurrent); the vector clock [0,0,1] vs [1,0,0] reveals it.
max skew 2·ρ·T = 129.60 s
step 0 / 6
Happens-before a→b ⇒ L[a]<L[b], but not the converse — equal or even ordered Lamport values say nothing about concurrency. The vector clock makes causality exact: a→b ⇔ V[a]≤V[b] componentwise and V[a]≠V[b]; otherwise a‖b.

③ Timing model — synchronous vs asynchronous vs partial synchrony (FLP)

What to do: pick a timing model. Synchronous: bounded delay → timeouts detect crashes, consensus is possible. Asynchronous: unbounded delay → a slow node is indistinguishable from a dead one, and FLP says deterministic consensus is impossible. Partial synchrony (what Raft/Paxos actually assume): a bound holds eventually.

④ Gold check — quorum thresholds, OM messages, clock traces recomputed live

What this proves: the page re-runs the quorum math (2f+1, 3f+1), the OM message-count recurrence, and the identical Lamport + vector-clock traces as distributed_systems.py GOLD CHECK. The green check: OK below confirms the JS numbers match the .py exactly.
check: … JS quorum thresholds, OM message counts, and Lamport/vector traces match distributed_systems.py GOLD CHECK.
